sound_type: deep resonant bugling -- produced by extra-long trachea coiled within the sternum, 200-1500 Hz, brassy commanding call audible at 1-3 km, the most powerful avian voice in North America, E=6 maximum energy, individual power not flock-driven
ecosystem: Wetland, resident/winter. Breeds on large freshwater marshes, lakes, and rivers in northern US and Canada. Year-round resident populations in PNW. Winters on open water in lowlands. Skagit Valley mixed flocks with Tundra Swan.
season: Year-round (resident populations) and winter visitor (migratory populations from Alaska/BC). Winter peak November-March in the Skagit Valley. Bugling heard throughout residency. Breeding pairs territorial in spring/summer.

coordinates.description: Reference for Skagit Valley wintering habitat. Fir Island area. Mixed flocks of Trumpeter and Tundra Swans in agricultural fields and shallow wetlands.

taxonomy_note.description: ELEVENTH consecutive Anatidae (118-128). Extends the all-time family streak. E=6 maximum energy returns to SPS axis. North America's largest native bird by weight. Conservation triumph: from fewer than 100 birds (1935) to approximately 63,000 today. Completes comparison pair with Tundra Swan (degree 127). Second swan in the engine.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (11th consecutive -- ALL-TIME RECORD EXTENDED, streak ongoing)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae: the most represented family by far. Eleven consecutive at degrees 118-128, plus earlier entries.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cygnus from Latin/Greek for 'swan.' buccinator from Latin buccinare ('to trumpet, to sound the horn') -- 'the trumpeter.' The name directly describes the deep bugling call produced by the tracheal resonating chamber.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cygnus -- second entry. Tundra Swan (127) and Trumpeter Swan (128). Both North American swans documented in consecutive degrees.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes. Eleventh consecutive Anatidae. The family streak continues.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at eleventh consecutive. ALL-TIME RECORD EXTENDS. The streak shows extraordinary family diversity: stream specialist to wetland giant, smallest duck to largest bird.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 200-1,500 Hz. Notably low for a bird. The extra-long trachea produces rich low harmonics. Distinctly lower than Tundra Swan (500-3,000 Hz).
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-800 Hz. The deep fundamental and first harmonics carry the most acoustic energy. The 'brassy' quality comes from the richness of low harmonics.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Bugling call: 200-1,500 Hz. Alarm call: sharper, higher. Contact call: softer versions of the bugle. The bugle is the defining sound.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single bugle: 1-3 seconds. Given in series of 2-5 calls. Accompanied by wing-flapping or head-bobbing displays. Territorial bugling may continue intermittently for minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: DEEP RESONANT BUGLE: Low-pitched, brassy, trumpet-like. Produced by extra-long trachea (over 1 meter) coiled within the keel of the sternum. The tracheal loop acts as a resonating tube. The call has been compared to a French horn or distant trumpet.
song_metrics.note_types: Territorial bugle (primary, deep resonant), alarm bugle (sharper, more urgent), contact call (softer bugle variation), pair duet (synchronized bugling between mates), wing-slap display (physical accompaniment to vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Series of 2-5 bugles with 1-3 second gaps. Pairs often bugle in duet, with calls overlapping. Unlike the Tundra Swan's uncoordinated flock baying, Trumpeter pair duetting is semi-coordinated.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep. Brassy. Resonant. Commanding. The tracheal loop produces a rich, horn-like quality fundamentally different from the Tundra Swan's whistling, quavering timbre. The sound has physical presence -- felt as much as heard.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6. MAXIMUM. A single Trumpeter Swan bugling can be heard at 1-3 km in favorable conditions. The loudest sound produced by any North American bird. Does not require flock amplification. Individual anatomy produces E=6.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate. Bugles given at 2-5 second intervals in series. Not rapid-fire like woodpecker drumming. The tempo is deliberate, commanding -- each bugle is a statement.
song_metrics.context: Territorial defense (primary), pair bonding (duetting), alarm (urgent bugling), general communication. The bugle announces presence, ownership, and status. Breeding pairs bugle at territory boundaries. Winter flocks bugle during feeding and flight.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The bugle is largely determined by tracheal anatomy (innate) rather than learned vocalization. Consistent across populations.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP RESONANT BUGLING -- E=6 MAXIMUM ENERGY. Extra-long trachea coiled in sternum. 200-1,500 Hz. Audible 1-3 km. Most powerful avian voice in N. America. North America's LARGEST NATIVE BIRD (up to 17.2 kg, 250 cm wingspan). Conservation triumph: <100 (1935) to ~63,000. ELEVENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-128). Completes SWAN COMPARISON PAIR with Tundra (127): deep bugling (E=6, individual) vs. high baying (E=5, flock). Buccinator = 'the trumpeter.'

plumage.overall: Adult: entirely white plumage. Solid black bill -- NO yellow loral spot (unlike Tundra Swan). Legs and feet black. Juvenile: gray-brown plumage, pinkish bill becoming darker. Larger and heavier than Tundra Swan in all dimensions.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: NO YELLOW LORAL SPOT (vs. Tundra's usually present yellow spot). Straight or slightly convex bill profile (vs. Tundra's concave). More angular, wedge-shaped head (vs. Tundra's rounded). Longer, heavier bill. Larger overall size.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Adults white year-round. Juveniles gray-brown in first winter, molting to white by second year. Some adults show rusty staining on head/neck from feeding in iron-rich waters.
plumage.flight_pattern: Long neck extended. Slow, powerful wingbeats. V-formation in flocks. Deep bugling calls during flight. Larger and heavier than Tundra in direct comparison.

physical_description.overall: NORTH AMERICA'S LARGEST NATIVE BIRD. Body length 138-165 cm (54-65 inches). Wingspan 185-250 cm (73-98 inches). Weight 7.1-17.2 kg (15.7-38 lbs). Males average ~11 kg, females ~9 kg.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: No yellow loral spot. Straight/convex bill profile. Angular head shape. Largest size. Deep bugling voice. In mixed Skagit Valley flocks, direct comparison with Tundra is the best identification approach.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the deep, brassy bugling -- unmistakably lower-pitched and more commanding than Tundra's high whistling. Look for: no yellow at bill base, straight bill profile, angular head, overall larger size. In mixed flocks at Fir Island, voice is the fastest separator.

nodes.title: Deep Resonant Bugling -- E=6 Maximum Energy
nodes.description: Extra-long trachea (over 1 meter) coiled in sternum acts as resonating chamber. 200-1,500 Hz. Audible 1-3 km. Most powerful avian voice in North America. E=6 through individual anatomy, not flock amplification. Buccinator = 'the trumpeter.'

nodes.title: Conservation Triumph -- <100 to 63,000
nodes.description: Near-extinction in 1935 (fewer than 100 in contiguous US). Red Rock Lakes NWR established. Reintroduction programs across historic range. Eight decades of recovery to approximately 63,000. One of the greatest conservation achievements in North American wildlife history.

nodes.title: North America's Largest Native Bird
nodes.description: Up to 17.2 kg (38 lbs), 250 cm (8+ feet) wingspan. Largest nest of any N. American bird (3.5 m diameter). Size drives the sound: large body, long trachea, powerful lungs. The largest bird produces the deepest, loudest voice.

nodes.title: Swan Comparison Pair Complete (127-128)
nodes.description: Tundra (127, high baying, E=5, flock, yellow spot, smaller, ~170K) vs. Trumpeter (128, deep bugling, E=6, individual, no spot, largest, ~63K). Collective vs. individual acoustic strategies. Fourth comparison pair in SPS. Mirrors Hairy/Downy WP (110-111) and Barrow's/Common Goldeneye (123-124).

nodes.title: Eleventh Consecutive Anatidae (118-128) -- Record Extended
nodes.description: Harlequin (118) through Trumpeter (128). Eleven consecutive. Energy arc: E=5, E=4, E=4, E=3, E=3, E=3, E=4, E=3, E=4, E=5, E=6. Maximum energy at the climax. Stream specialist to wetland giant. Smallest duck (Bufflehead) to largest bird (Trumpeter).

cross_references.relationship: SWAN PAIR COMPLETE! Tundra (127, high baying, E=5, flock-driven, yellow spot, smaller) vs. Trumpeter (128, deep bugling, E=6, individual power, no spot, largest native bird). Collective vs. individual. Whistling vs. commanding.
STREAK ARC! Harlequin (118, E=5) to Trumpeter (128, E=6). Eleven consecutive Anatidae. From torrential stream to freshwater wetland. Energy arc: E=5 start to E=6 climax.
E=6 COMPARISON! Pileated drum (mechanical, percussive, bill strikes, E=6) vs. Trumpeter bugle (vocal, resonant, tracheal chamber, E=6). Two maximum-energy sounds, two production mechanisms.
SIZE SPECTRUM! Bufflehead (122, ~450g, smallest diving duck) to Trumpeter (128, ~11kg avg, largest native bird). 24x weight difference within the same Anatidae streak.
RECORD EXTENDED! Picidae held 9 consecutive (107-115). Anatidae broke it at 10 (Tundra Swan, 127). Now extended to 11 (Trumpeter Swan, 128). The margin grows.
